content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 8 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in 1 Corinthians Chapter 8 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 8:1 -- "puffs up / makes us feel important" and "builds up / strengthens the church"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "we know that 'we all have knowledge.' The knowledge puffs up, but the love builds up" | physiomai as "puffs up"; oikodomeo as "builds up"; articles preserved |
| LOGOS | "we know that 'all of us possess knowledge.' This 'knowledge' puffs up, but love builds up" | Adds quotation marks around "knowledge" to flag Corinthian slogan |
| PNEUMA | "Yes, we know that 'we all have knowledge' about this issue. But while knowledge makes us feel important, it is love that strengthens the church" | physiomai as "makes us feel important"; oikodomeo as "strengthens the church"; adds "about this issue" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Puffs up" for physiomai should be preserved -- it is Paul's signature metaphor for the Corinthian problem (used in 4:6, 18, 19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4), and consistency across the letter matters. PNEUMA's "makes us feel important" interprets accurately but loses the recurring inflation metaphor. "Builds up" (oikodomeo) is the correct architectural metaphor; PNEUMA's "strengthens the church" adds "the church" and changes the metaphor from construction to empowerment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:2 -- "imagines that he knows / claims to know all the answers / thinks he knows"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If anyone thinks he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know" | dokeo as "thinks"; ginosko as "know" |
| LOGOS | "If anyone imagines that he knows something, he does not yet know as he ought to know" | dokeo as "imagines" |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who claims to know all the answers doesn't really know very much" | Adds "all the answers"; "doesn't really know very much" paraphrases |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Imagines" for dokeo effectively conveys the slight unreality of the claim (one supposes one knows). PNEUMA's "claims to know all the answers" adds "all the answers" and "doesn't really know very much" replaces Paul's precise formulation ("does not yet know as he ought to know") -- Paul's point is not that the person lacks knowledge but that they lack the right manner of knowing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:3 -- "he is known by God / God recognizes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone loves God, this [one] is known by him" | ginosko passive: "is known by him" |
| LOGOS | "if anyone loves God, he is known by God" | Makes "God" explicit |
| PNEUMA | "the person who loves God is the one whom God recognizes" | ginosko as "recognizes" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Known by God" preserves the divine passive -- the remarkable shift from human knowing (v.2) to being known by God (v.3). PNEUMA's "recognizes" implies acknowledgment or approval, which narrows the relational depth of ginosko. To be "known by God" in Paul's framework means to be in intimate relationship with God.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:4 -- "no real existence / nothing in the world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "an idol [is] nothing in [the] world" | eidolon ouden en kosmo -- literal |
| LOGOS | "an idol has no real existence" | Interprets ouden as ontological nonexistence |
| PNEUMA | "an idol is not really a god and that there is only one God" | Splits and expands; adds "not really a god" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Has no real existence" captures the ontological claim: an idol has no being as a god. EMET's "nothing in the world" is literal but ambiguous (does it mean "worthless" or "nonexistent"?). PNEUMA's expansion adds "not really a god" which reframes the ontological claim as a relational one.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:5 -- "so-called gods" and "some people actually worship"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth -- as indeed there are many 'gods' and many 'lords'" | legomenoi theoi as "so-called gods" |
| LOGOS | "For although there may be so-called gods in heaven or on earth -- as indeed there are many 'gods' and many 'lords'" | Same, with concessive "although" |
| PNEUMA | "There may be so-called gods both in heaven and on earth, and some people actually worship many gods and many lords" | Adds "some people actually worship"; changes observation to behavioral description |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul's parenthetical "as indeed there are many gods and many lords" acknowledges the phenomenological reality of pagan worship without endorsing the ontological reality of the gods. PNEUMA's "some people actually worship" shifts from Paul's complex theological statement to a sociological observation. The Greek simply says there are (eisin) many gods and lords -- Paul leaves the exact nature of their "being" ambiguous (they exist as objects of worship, even if they are not truly divine).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:6 -- "from whom are all things and for whom we exist / who created everything and we live for him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "one God, the Father, from whom [are] all [things] and we for him, and one Lord, Yeshua Christos, through whom [are] all [things] and we through him" | Preserves compressed prepositional structure: ex hou...eis auton...di' hou...di' autou |
| LOGOS | "yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom are all things and for whom we exist, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things and through whom we exist" | Supplies "we exist" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "there is one God, the Father, who created everything, and we live for him. And there is one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom God made everything and through whom we have been given life" | "created everything" for "from whom all things"; "live for him" for "for whom we exist"; "made everything" and "given life" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. This verse is one of the most theologically dense in Paul's letters -- a christological expansion of the Jewish Shema. The prepositional distinctions (ex/eis for the Father, dia for Christ) carry enormous theological weight and must be preserved precisely. LOGOS does this while supplying "we exist" for English clarity. PNEUMA's rendering collapses the prepositions: "created" replaces "from whom" (which denotes origin/source, not just creation), "made" replaces "through whom" (which denotes agency/mediation). Most significantly, PNEUMA's "through whom God made everything" inserts "God" as an explicit subject not in the Greek, and "through whom we have been given life" replaces the broader "through whom we exist."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. This verse is too theologically precise for dynamic paraphrase.

---

## 8:7 -- "through former association with idols / accustomed to thinking of idols as being real" and "conscience, being weak, is defiled"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "some, by the habit until now of the idol, eat as [food] sacrificed to an idol, and the conscience of them, being weak, is defiled" | synetheia as "habit"; moluno as "defiled" |
| LOGOS | "some, through former association with idols, eat food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled" | synetheia as "former association" |
| PNEUMA | "Some are accustomed to thinking of idols as being real, so when they eat food that has been offered to idols, they think of it as the worship of real gods, and their weak consciences are violated" | Major expansion; adds "thinking of idols as being real"; "worship of real gods"; "violated" for moluno |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Former association with idols" captures synetheia (habitual familiarity) effectively. PNEUMA's expansion adds interpretive material: "thinking of idols as being real" and "the worship of real gods" are reasonable inferences but not in the Greek. "Violated" for moluno (defile, stain) shifts the metaphor from ritual pollution to rights violation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:8 -- "commend us to God / win God's approval"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "food will not commend us to God. Neither if we do not eat are we lacking, nor if we eat do we abound" | paristemi as "commend"; hystero as "lacking"; perisseuo as "abound" |
| LOGOS | "Food will not commend us to God. We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do" | "no worse off / no better off" interprets the contrast |
| PNEUMA | "It's true that we can't win God's approval by what we eat. We don't lose anything if we don't eat it, and we don't gain anything if we do" | "win God's approval" for paristemi; "don't lose/gain anything" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Commend" for paristemi (present, recommend) is more precise than PNEUMA's "win God's approval" (which implies a transactional relationship). LOGOS's "no worse off / no better off" effectively renders the hystero/perisseuo contrast in accessible English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:9 -- "right / freedom"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "watch lest somehow this right of yours becomes a stumbling block to the weak [ones]" | exousia as "right" |
| LOGOS | "take care that this right of yours does not somehow become a stumbling block to the weak" | exousia as "right" |
| PNEUMA | "you must be careful so that your freedom does not cause others with a weaker conscience to stumble" | exousia as "freedom"; adds "others with a weaker conscience" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Right" for exousia is more precise in this context than "freedom" (PNEUMA). Paul is discussing the Corinthians' exousia (authority, right, power) to eat idol-food, not their eleutheria (freedom) in general. The distinction matters: a right can be voluntarily limited; freedom is a broader concept.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:10 -- "encouraged / built up" (ironic oikodomeo)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will not the conscience of him, being weak, be built up to eat the [things] sacrificed to idols?" | oikodomeo as "built up" (preserving the ironic use) |
| LOGOS | "will he not be encouraged, if his conscience is weak, to eat food offered to idols?" | oikodomeo as "encouraged" |
| PNEUMA | "won't they be encouraged to violate their conscience by eating food that has been offered to an idol?" | oikodomeo as "encouraged"; adds "violate their conscience" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, but EMET's "built up" deserves special note. Paul uses oikodomeo ("build up") ironically here -- the same word he used positively in v.1 ("love builds up"). The "strong" believer's example "builds up" the weak believer to eat idol-food, but this is destructive "building." LOGOS's "encouraged" loses this ironic echo. However, "encouraged" is clearer for English readers. A footnote on the ironic oikodomeo is warranted.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with EMET's ironic observation noted for a footnote.

---

## 8:11 -- "destroyed / destroyed" and "by your knowledge / because of your superior knowledge"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] being weak is destroyed by the knowledge of you -- the brother for whom Christos died" | apollymi as "destroyed"; "knowledge of you" |
| LOGOS | "by your knowledge this weak person is destroyed, the brother for whom Christ died" | Same core |
| PNEUMA | "because of your superior knowledge, a weak believer is destroyed -- someone for whom Christ died" | Adds "superior"; "believer" for adelphos; "someone" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "By your knowledge" is sufficient -- PNEUMA's "superior" adds a qualifier not in the Greek. "Brother" (adelphos) should be preserved rather than PNEUMA's "believer," as the familial language is integral to Paul's appeal: you are destroying your brother, not an abstract fellow believer.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 8:13 -- "never eat meat / never eat meat again as long as I live"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will never eat meat into the age, lest I cause the brother of me to stumble" | ou me...eis ton aiona as "never...into the age" (emphatic double negative + eternal scope) |
| LOGOS | "if food makes my brother stumble, I will never eat meat, lest I make my brother stumble" | "never eat meat" |
| PNEUMA | "if what I eat causes another believer to sin, I will never eat meat again as long as I live -- for I don't want to cause another believer to stumble" | Adds "again as long as I live"; "I don't want to"; "another believer" for "brother" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "into the age" is the literal rendering of eis ton aiona (forever, in perpetuity), which LOGOS captures with "never" and PNEUMA expands to "never...again as long as I live." PNEUMA's expansion is interpretively accurate but verbose. PNEUMA also adds "I don't want to" as a motivational explanation not in the text. Again, PNEUMA renders adelphos as "another believer" rather than "brother."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA replaces Paul's recurring metaphors with fresh language | vv.1, 10 | "puffs up" becomes "makes us feel important"; ironic oikodomeo lost |
| PNEUMA expands compressed theological formulas | v.6 | Christological Shema paraphrased; prepositions collapsed |
| PNEUMA adds interpretive qualifiers | vv.2, 5, 7, 11, 13 | "all the answers," "actually worship," "superior," "as long as I live" |
| PNEUMA replaces "brother" with "believer" | vv.11, 13 | Loses familial ecclesiology |
| EMET preserves ironic and intertextual connections | vv.1, 10, 13 | physiomai consistency; ironic oikodomeo; "into the age" |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Throughout | LOGOS as base text |
| Theological precision in key terms | vv.3, 4, 6, 9 | ginosko, ouden, prepositions, exousia vs. eleutheria |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Chapter 8 is theologically compact, anchored by the christological Shema of v.6 and governed by the knowledge/love contrast of v.1. The most consequential divergence is PNEUMA's handling of v.6, where the paraphrase collapses the precise prepositional theology (ex, eis, dia) that distinguishes the Father's and Christ's roles within the one divine identity. This verse is too theologically precise for dynamic rendering. PNEUMA's consistent replacement of "brother" (adelphos) with "believer" throughout the chapter weakens Paul's appeal -- the familial language is integral to the stumbling-block argument. If the person you are harming is your brother, the obligation is personal and inescapable. EMET's most valuable contribution in this chapter is the observation of ironic oikodomeo in v.10, which all translations should flag. The chapter demonstrates that in Paul's most compressed theological passages, the LOGOS/SOPHIA formal approach best preserves the argument's structure and force.

---
